payload.summary: Opening persuasion-audit session. Requesting psychology-agent consultation on six questions about unratified.org's psychological architecture — covering framing, tone, trust, and persuasion effectiveness.

context.mission: Advocate for U.S. ratification of the International Covenant on Economic, Social and Cultural Rights (ICESCR). Site covers legal analysis, economic impact, legislative pathways, and voter action guides.
context.audience: Five-lens model: voter (default), politician, developer, educator, researcher. Each lens has distinct framing, reading level targets, and call-to-action structure.
context.ai_authorship: Site built by unratified-agent (Claude Code) + Kashif Shah. AI disclosure banner on every page. Four-part author attribution on all blog posts.
context.psq_history: psq-scoring session (turns 1-9) established PSQ-Lite formula TE+TC+AR (D087). AR replaces HI due to construct mismatch. Phase 1 validation passed on 5-text corpus.
context.recent_work: D029: prohibition-entitlement reframing ('No person should...') applied to 23 passages. D028: five-lens persona copy with distinct lensFraming frontmatter on all MDX content.

questions.label: Persuasion framing validation
questions.question: The site uses a five-lens model (voter, politician, educator, researcher, developer) to frame identical underlying facts for different audiences. Does this model map to psychologically distinct cognitive profiles? Specifically: are we activating the right epistemic modes per persona (System 1 / System 2, identity-protective vs. accuracy-motivated reasoning), or does any lens risk triggering defensive processing in its target audience?

questions.label: PSQ-Lite full site scan
questions.question: We have PSQ-Lite (TE+TC+AR) validated on a 5-text corpus. We would like to extend the scan to the full site's major pages — the five persona pages, the /why, /gap, /how, /action pages, the /connection/* case studies, and a sample of covenant articles. Which pages, if any, show adversarial register (AR) signals that could alienate the audiences we most need to reach? Is there a tone gradient across the site that warrants reordering or rewriting?

questions.label: AI authorship disclosure effects
questions.question: The site discloses AI authorship prominently: a review banner on every page, 'Built by Claude Code + Kashif Shah' in footers, and four-part author attribution on all blog posts. What does the psychology literature say about AI disclosure effects on perceived credibility and persuasion? Are we over-disclosing (triggering algorithmic aversion), under-disclosing (losing trust when readers notice), or is the framing approximately right? Is 'Built by' versus 'Written by' a meaningful distinction to readers?

questions.label: Prohibition framing validation
questions.question: D029 applied 'No person should be left without...' / 'No worker should face...' / 'No community should lose...' framing to 23 passages across 8 files, replacing abstract legal language with prohibition-entitlement framing. This was based on a rights-language analysis rather than empirical persuasion research. Is this framing psychologically grounded? Does prohibition framing ('No X should Y') outperform positive rights framing ('Everyone has a right to Y') for the specific audiences we target — particularly voters skeptical of rights expansion?

questions.label: Senator contact psychology
questions.question: The site includes a template letters page with eight constituent contact templates (voter, politician/colleague, educator, researcher, developer personas; FOIA request; media inquiry; civil society coalition letter). What psychological principles govern constituent contact effectiveness? Specifically: commitment/consistency, social proof, identity salience, and loss framing — are these templates using the right psychological levers for their target recipients? What is the psychology literature's best estimate of which contact strategies produce the highest conversion to legislative action?

questions.label: Motivated reasoning mitigation
questions.question: A significant segment of the target political audience holds prior commitments that predict resistance to ICESCR ratification: pro-sovereignty instincts, skepticism of UN institutions, and concerns about 'big government' economic intervention. These priors activate identity-protective cognition rather than accuracy-motivated reasoning. How should advocacy content be structured to minimize defensive processing in this segment without abandoning substantive rigor? Does the site's current information architecture (discriminator analysis, four-scarcities model, quantitative consolidation) help or hurt with identity-protective readers?

payload.response_format: Address questions in priority order (high first). For each: state the applicable psychological construct(s), summarize the relevant empirical literature (with confidence), give a direct assessment of the site's current approach, and recommend specific changes if warranted. PSQ-Lite scoring on Q-B is welcome but not required in turn 1 — scoping the scan is sufficient.

action_gate.gate_note: All six questions are independent. Psychology-agent may address any subset in any order.

epistemic_flags: Q-D and Q-F involve empirical claims about persuasion effectiveness — confidence in our current approach is low (intuition-based); psychology-agent's literature grounding is the primary reason for this consultation
